pass1_value.note: Close uses API Keys; keys are supplied via HTTP Basic auth (API key as username, blank password) or an Authorization header with Basic <base64(API_KEY:)>.

final_value.note: Close API authentication requires an API key and uses HTTP Basic Authentication: use your API key as the username and leave the password blank (e.g., curl -u "YOUR_API_KEY:" ...). Docs also indicate you can authenticate via an Authorization header using a Basic token.

pass1_value.note: API keys are created by a logged-in user in Settings -> API Keys in the Close dashboard; documentation shows a user can create keys from the UI. Docs note that if you don't see API Keys, organization permissions may restrict visibility (admin settings), but generally keys are self-service from the dashboard.

final_value.note: API keys can be created and managed in the Close app via Settings → API Keys (New API Key). If a user doesn’t see API Keys in Settings, they may lack permission (e.g., admin-only), but credential creation is otherwise self-serve.

pass1_value.note: Public REST docs exist and API keys can be created self-serve in the dashboard, so a developer can build integrations today. Only potential blocker is organization-level permission settings may prevent some users from creating keys (requires admin in that org).

final_value.note: Public Close REST API docs exist; authentication is API-key-based HTTP Basic auth; API keys are created in-app (subject to org permissions).
